technical_subject.key_spec: ANSI/ASHRAE reference cycle diagram — four-way valve slide mechanism
technical_subject.diagram_prompt: Technical exploded schematic: four-way reversing valve body, slide piston, capillary tubes to indoor and outdoor coils, solenoid pilot; cyan schematic lines on navy field, part callouts, accurate topology not decorative

sources.citation: ASHRAE Handbook — Fundamentals, Chapter 9: Refrigeration Cycles and Systems
U.S. DOE Energy Saver — Heat Pump Systems
Carrier Service Manual — Four-Way Reversing Valve (illustrative teardown reference)

beats.speech_text: Your heat pump heats and cools from one outdoor box. The part that flips the whole cycle lives inside this valve — and most homeowners have never seen it.
A four-way valve routes hot refrigerant gas either to the indoor coil for heat, or to the outdoor coil for cooling. A slide piston inside the body swaps those paths in milliseconds.
ASHRAE documents the standard vapor-compression cycle. Manufacturer teardowns show the slide-and-capillary pilot that moves the piston when the thermostat calls for mode change.
Watch the payoff: refrigerant enters here, the slide shifts, and the flow diagram inverts — same compressor, opposite job. One valve, two seasons.
beats.diagram_prompt: Exploded four-way heat pump reversing valve: brass body, slide piston, solenoid pilot, capillary lines to coils; labeled flow arrows for heating vs cooling modes; cyan-on-navy schematic

beats.speech_text: When this valve sticks, you get heat in summer or ice in winter — and a service call that costs more than the part. Knowing the mechanism is knowing what failed.
